exemplar.title: How did realist and liberalist motivations and considerations influence Russiaâs abstention vote for UN Security Council Resolution 1973?

detailedExemplarData.title: How did realist and liberalist motivations and considerations influence Russiaâs abstention vote for UN Security Council Resolution 1973?

strengths: Research question is precisely formulated and guides each section, ensuring sustained focus.
Topic is communicated accurately with clear definitions of key terms and consistent signposting.
Argumentation is tightly linked back to the research question throughout the essay.
weaknesses: Methodology is appropriate but only implicitly described; lacks an explicit methodology section detailing source selection.
Limited reflection on research limitations or methodological choices in the conclusion.
Minor lapses in explicit signposting of methodological rationale reduce transparency.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Although the student demonstrates a very clear, focused research question and integrates it throughout the essay (5-6 band), the absence of an explicit methodology section and limited reflection on research limitations prevent a perfect score. The work sits at the upper end of the 3-4 band for methodology but overall merits a strong 5/6 for Focus and Method.

strengths: Terminology and concepts from International Relations are used accurately and consistently throughout.
Source material is clearly relevant and woven smoothly into the analysis, demonstrating effective use of evidence.
Demonstrates coherent and in-depth knowledge of both the Libyan crisis and realist/liberal theoretical frameworks.
weaknesses: Occasional reliance on non-scholarly blogs for factual assertions without triangulation.
Procedural details of UNSC voting could be more explicitly linked to theoretical assumptions.
Omission of constructivist or Marxist perspectives limits the breadth of disciplinary engagement.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay shows clear, coherent knowledge of the topic and discipline, with precise and consistent use of terminology (5-6 band). Source application is almost always appropriate and relevant, meriting the top mark of 6/6 for Knowledge and Understanding despite minor lapses in source triangulation and disciplinary breadth.

strengths: Research draws on a broad, authoritative range of sources directly relevant to the question, balancing multiple perspectives.
Analysis moves beyond description, weighing realist versus liberal explanations and supporting interim conclusions logically.
The concluding synthesis effectively integrates political, economic, and diplomatic motivations into a balanced narrative.
weaknesses: Analysis would benefit from deeper empirical data or quantitative evidence to substantiate key claims.
Source bias and reliability are noted only implicitly; more explicit critical evaluation of each sourceâs limitations is needed.
Some thematic sections lack explicit theoretical contrasts, reducing depth of critical evaluation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The essay demonstrates good research quality and solid, well-supported analysis and evaluation, placing it firmly in the 7-9 band. While the work is strong and coherent, it does not consistently reach the level of exceptional, hence a score of 8/12 for Critical Thinking.

strengths: Clear and appropriate overall structure with comprehensive table of contents and consistent heading hierarchy.
Layout conventions (fonts, spacing, headings) are applied correctly, supporting readability.
Transitions and sub-heading signposts guide the reader effectively through analytical sections.
weaknesses: Bibliography formatting is inconsistent, with non-alphabetised entries and duplicate references.
Some paragraphs are overly long, which can impede reader engagement and emphasis.
Minor citation anomalies and placeholder symbols in references detract from professional presentation.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets all criteria for the top band (3-4) with clear structure and layout, despite small formatting inconsistencies. Therefore, it merits the maximum 4/4 for Presentation.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay moves beyond narrative to weigh realist versus liberal explanations, assesses multiple motives (sovereignty, regime-change fears, regional diplomacy, limited interests) and integrates evidence to support reasoned judgements. Interim conclusions at the end of each subsection feed logically into the final synthesis.  

Most claims are substantiated, and contrasting perspectives (e.g., Arab Leagueâs stance versus AUâs) are acknowledged. Nevertheless, some key pointsâsuch as economic calculations or precedent fearsâcould be underpinned by deeper empirical data or alternative theoretical frames, preventing the analysis from reaching the âexcellentâ threshold.  

The depth and logical coherence align with the 7-9 band for analysis; a score of 8 recognises its solid, well-supportedâthough not consistently exceptionalâanalytical quality.

The student assembles a broad, largely authoritative body of evidenceâincluding UN primary sources, official statements, academic monographs and journal articlesâdemonstrating deliberate effort to secure materials directly relevant to the research question.  

Sources are generally current and appropriate; the essay balances Western and Russian perspectives and incorporates regional voices. While evaluation of source reliability is implicit rather than explicit, the overall selection underpins the argument effectively, with only sporadic reliance on less scholarly outlets.  

These characteristics match the âgood research mostly relevant to the research questionâ description, placing the work in the 7-9 band; a mid-band score of 8 reflects strong, though not exemplary, research quality.

The essay presents a logical, reader-friendly structure: clear title page, detailed table of contents, thematic sectioning (âIntroduction,â dual analytical chapters, conclusion), and appropriate use of headings and sub-headings that mirror the research question. Transitional phrases guide the reader effectively from one argument to the next.  

Minor issuesâoccasional lengthy paragraphs that could be divided for emphasisâdo not impede understanding. All required components (abstract not required under current guide, bibliography, word count) are included.  

Given the overall clarity and effectiveness, the work meets the top 3-4 band for structure; a score of 4 is merited.

The essay demonstrates clear command of both the Libyan crisis and the theoretical frames of realism and liberalism. Historical background, UN procedures, and Russiaâs broader geopolitical interests are accurately described and linked to scholarly interpretations.  

Use of sources such as UN primary records, academic analyses (e.g., Dagi, Henriksen & Larssen) and official Russian statements showcases effective engagement with the discipline. Minor gapsâlimited reference to alternative theories like constructivism or Marxismâslightly reduce the depth of disciplinary breadth.  

Overall coherence and mostly effective sourcing justify placement in the 5-6 band, with a score of 5 to reflect occasional areas where knowledge could have been extended further.

The discussion is structured around clear thematic questions, culminating in a reasoned conclusion that synthesises realist and liberal motives. The student evaluates competing explanations (e.g., concern for sovereignty vs. desire to avoid alienating regional partners) and acknowledges limitations, such as the roles of Medvedev versus Putin and the AUâs position.  

Evaluation is present but not always deeply critical; for example, source biases and alternative theoretical lenses are noted only briefly, and quantitative evidence is limited. Some arguments could be probed further for counter-arguments or implications.  

These qualities fit the âgood reasoned argument with partial critical evaluationâ descriptor in the 7-9 band. A score of 7 reflects solid discussion and evaluation, albeit with room for more penetrating critique.

Consistent fonts, spacing and heading hierarchy contribute to professional presentation. The table format for contents and the use of HTML tags (<b>) are unconventional but functionally clear; page numbers and required formal elements are present.  

Minor inconsistencies appear in bibliography formatting and occasional line-break anomalies, yet citations are generally complete and allow sources to be located.  

These small lapses keep the work from perfection, but overall layout considerations are accurately applied, fully satisfying the 3-4 band with a top score of 4.

Throughout, the student employs International Relations terminologyââsovereignty,â âR2P,â âregime change,â âmultilateralism,â âhard power,â and ânational interestââaccurately and consistently. Concepts from realism and liberalism are correctly differentiated and applied to concrete evidence.  

Definitions are generally concise and situated at the point of first use, supporting reader understanding without interrupting flow. Misuse or ambiguity of terms is negligible.  

This precise, sustained deployment of disciplinary language meets the highest descriptor for the 5-6 band; a full score of 6 is warranted.

The studentâs topicâRussiaâs abstention on UNSCR 1973â is stated unambiguously from the title page onward and is introduced with a succinct, well-contextualised overview of the Libyan crisis, R2P and Security Council procedures.  

Key terms (e.g., âno-fly zone,â âResponsibility to Protect,â âabstentionâ) are clearly defined, and the relevance of examining realist versus liberal explanations is explicitly justified, giving the reader an immediate and comprehensive understanding of what is being investigated and why it matters.  

The essay maintains this clarity throughout, consistently signposting section aims (e.g., âWhy not approveâ¦â / âWhy not vetoâ¦â) so that the reader can track how each part contributes to answering the research question. These features place the work firmly in the 5-6 band for topic communication and explanation.

The essay employs an appropriate range of primary documents (UNSC debates, press releases, leadersâ statements) and secondary scholarship, indicating a generally sound methodological base.  

However, the student provides only implicit insight into how sources were identified, selected or evaluated for reliability and bias; there is no dedicated methodology section detailing research choices or limitations (beyond a brief mention in the conclusion). Consequently, evidence of a consciously designed research strategy is present but not fully articulated.  

These characteristics correspond to a mostly completeâyet not fully explicitâmethodology, meriting the 3-4 band at the upper level.

The research questionââHow did realist and liberalist motivations and considerations influence Russiaâs abstention vote for UN Security Council Resolution 1973?ââis precise, focused and allows for systematic investigation.  

Throughout the essay, every main section (âWhy not approveâ¦,â âWhy not vetoâ¦â) explicitly links evidence back to realist or liberal logics, demonstrating a strong, sustained focus on addressing the question. Occasional minor lapses in explicit signposting do not undermine the overall tight alignment between discussion and question.  

Given the clarity, specificity and sustained integration of the question into the argument, the strand meets the descriptors for the 5-6 band.

The student integrates more than thirty sources, including UN documents, scholarly works (e.g., Baldwin, Martin), reputable news outlets, and some think-tank reports. Source material is almost always germane to the points being made, and quotations are woven smoothly into the analysis.  

Yet a handful of factual assertionsâsuch as oil-export figuresârest on single, less scholarly sources (e.g., a blog), and cross-referencing or triangulation is occasionally missing. These minor issues constrain absolute consistency but do not substantially detract from overall relevance and appropriateness.  

Given the breadth, variety and largely effective deployment of evidence, the work sits securely in the 5-6 band; a score of 5 reflects strong but not flawless application.

annotations.comment: The procedural overview of UNSC voting could be better integrated with theoretical frameworks; explicitly linking these details to realist or liberal assumptions would deepen conceptual coherence.
Key economic assertions on oil exports rely on nonâscholarly blogs without triangulation; consider using authoritative trade databases to verify figures and strengthen source credibility.
The introduction effectively contextualises UNSCR 1973 with accurate background on the Libyan crisis and R2P, demonstrating clear knowledge of the historical and disciplinary setting.
The sub-heading signposting (âMethodological concernsâ¦â) effectively guides the reader to the topic focus, strengthening the organizational clarity of the analytical chapters.
The bibliography heading is present but the entries beneath display inconsistent formatting and entry order; adopt a single citation style (e.g., APA or MLA) and apply it uniformly.

The introduction lacks an explicit methodology section outlining how sources were selected or evaluated; adding a dedicated methodological description would strengthen transparency and research rigour.
The discussion of sovereignty and R2P demonstrates accurate use of key International Relations terminology, reflecting solid command of core concepts and correct application to the Libyan context.

The regime-change section offers persuasive examples but lacks deeper empirical data (e.g. economic metrics or polling) to substantiate the claims; integrating quantitative evidence would reinforce the analysis.
The analysis of regional bloc support (Arab League, GCC, OIC) demonstrates strong critical thinking by contextualising Russiaâs decision within broader geopolitical dynamics.
The conclusion recapitulates analysis effectively but does not reflect on research limitations or methodological choices; adding evaluative reflection would demonstrate metacognitive awareness.
The conclusion is clearly demarcated with a level-two heading, providing an immediate signal to the reader that synthesis and final judgments will follow.
The synthesis of political, economic, and diplomatic motivations in the concluding chapter reveals strong critical thinking, effectively weaving evidence into a balanced final narrative.

The analysis focuses on realism and liberalism but omits constructivist or Marxist perspectives that could offer alternative explanations; consider briefly addressing these to widen disciplinary breadth.
The transition to a new analytical subsection (âConcerns over regime changeâ) is smoothly signposted, maintaining a coherent thread and aiding reader orientation.

The research question is precisely formulated and prominently positioned on the title page, providing a clear focus that guides the entire inquiry and ensures consistent alignment of each section with the investigation aims.
The table of contents is comprehensive and clearly formatted, supporting easy navigation and demonstrating a well-planned structure that aligns with IB presentation expectations.
The consistent use of clear sub-headings throughout the analytical sections demonstrates effective application of layout conventions, aiding readability and navigation.

While the student notes methodological ambiguities, the section does not critically evaluate source bias or reliability; incorporating an assessment of each sourceâs strengths and limitations would enhance analytical depth.
The chapter shift to âWhy not vetoâ is clearly marked with a level-two heading, ensuring the structure consistently mirrors the research questionâs dual-chapter design.
The discussion notes regional endorsements but stops short of evaluating potential biases or strategic interests of these IGOs; assessing their motivations would deepen critical evaluation.

The comparative section on Syria vs Libya insightfully highlights realist priorities but lacks explicit theoretical contrast; clarify which realist and liberal assumptions account for Russiaâs differing responses.
Several paragraphs exceed a full page in length, which can impede reader engagement; consider dividing dense text into shorter paragraphs for emphasis and readability.
Each major analytical shift is accompanied by a clear level-three heading, reinforcing structure and supporting reader comprehension across multiple argument stages.
Inclusion of limitations acknowledges gaps and suggests directions for further research, demonstrating emerging evaluative depth and critical self-awareness.
The bibliography entries are not alphabetised and some URLs are incomplete or broken across lines; ensure correct ordering and full, stable links to improve presentation quality.
Some entries in the final bibliography page duplicate previous sources without clear differentiation; remove redundancies and cross-check to ensure each reference appears only once.
Repetitive use of placeholder symbols (e.g., â---.â) in the bibliography indicates missing author information; verify each entry to avoid ambiguity and maintain professional standards.
